0.text: CFI 092/2024 MAG Development Services Limited v (1) The Collection Club Restaurant Limited (2) Laurent Buisine (3) Hugo Valat

1.text: JANUARY 28, 2026 COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE - ORDERS

2.text: IN THE DUBAI INTERNATIONAL FINANCIAL CENTRE COURTS

3.text: IN THE COURT OF FIRST INSTANCE

8.text: (1) THE COLLECTION CLUB RESTAURANT LIMITED (2) LAURENT BUISINE (3) HUGO VALAT

10.text: ORDER WITH REASONS OF H.E. DEPUTY CHIEF JUSTICE ALI AL MADHANI

11.text: UPON the Claim Form being fi led on 10 December 2024 (the “Claim”)

12.text: AND UPON the Defendants’ Application No. CFI-092-2024/2 dated 7 October 2025, seeking an order for immediate judgment pursuant to Rule 24.1 of the DIFC Court Rules (“RDC”) (the “Application”)

13.text: AND UPON review of the Claimant’s evidence in answer dated 24 October 2025

14.text: AND UPON review of the Defendants’ evidence in reply dated 31 October 2025

15.text: AND UPON hearing counsel for the Claimant and counsel for the Defendants at the Application Hearing before H.E. Deputy Chief Justice Ali Al Madhani on 12 November 2025 (the “Hearing”)

17.text: AND UPON review of the submissions fi led on the Court fi le

19.text: 1. The Application is dismissed. The Claim is to proceed to trial to be determined alongside the main issues.

20.text: 2. Costs are awarded on the standard basis to be assessed by way of parties’ submissions, as follows:

21.text: (a) The Claimant shall fi le its costs submissions, to be no longer than 3 pages, within 5 working days from issue of this Order.

22.text: (b) The Defendants shall fi le their response within 5 working days thereafter.

23.text:
Issued by:   
Hayley Norton   
Assistant Registrar   
Date of issue: 28 January 2026   
At: 9am

25.text: 1. This Application is brought under RDC Part 24 by the Defendants to seek an order for immediate judgment to strike out the Second and Third Defendants (“D2” and “D3” respectively) from proceedings a...

26.text: 2. D2 and D3 are the shareholders and directors of the First Defendant (“D1”), which is a private company incorporated in the DIFC.

28.text: 3. On 15 August 2023, the Defendants entered into a lease agreement (the “Agreement”) with the Claimant for the fi t-out and renting of premises in the DIFC in preparation for the launch of the D1 com...

29.text: “However, we would like to highlight and agree that once the UAE Company License is issued and provided within the timeframe mentioned in the Agreement (no later than 3 months) an addendum to the Leas...

30.text: We appreciate your feedback and confi rmation regarding the above matter, to proceed with the execution of the Lease Agreement.” [emphasis added].

33.text: Further to your email below, Kindly note that we confirm the below, Please arrange to sign the Lease Agreement and send to us ASAP.” [emphasis added].

34.text: 4. On 17 August 2023, D2 and D3 returned the signed Agreement to the Claimant.

35.text: 5. On 28 and 31 August 2023, reminder emails from the Claimant were sent to the Defendants for the rental cheques to be arranged as the Agreement was active on the 15 August 2023

36.text:
6. In accordance with the Application, on 4 September 2023, the Licence was issued and backdated to   
6 June 2023.

37.text: 7. On 6 September 2023, the original Agreement signed by D2 and D3 on 17 August 2023 was returned to the Defendants with the Claimant’s signature. No formal amended Agreement with D2 and D3 removed, o...

38.text: 8. On or around 15 March 2024, D1 defaulted on its rental payment.

39.text: 9. On 24 June 2024, the Claimant was notifi ed that D1 intended to terminate the Agreement effective immediately.

40.text: 10. Between 16 July 2024 and 2 October 2024, negotiations were attempted to fi nd a commercially viable solution.

41.text: 11. On 16 September 2024, following unsuccessful negotiations, the Claimant served its own notice to terminate the Agreement.

42.text: 12. On 7 November 2024, D1 vacated the premises and returned the keys to the Claimant.

43.text: 13. On 5 December 2024, the Claimant served its Claim against the Defendants. The Defendants deny service of the Claim.

44.text: 14. On 10 March 2025, the Claimant applied for a Default Judgment pursuant to RDC 13.1. This was granted on 13 March 2025.

45.text: 15. On 27 March 2025 the Defendants applied to set aside the Default Judgment and sought a stay of enforcement proceedings.

46.text: 16. On 18 April 2025, a representative of the Claimant submitted a lease termination request.

47.text: 17. On 22 April 2025, by way of the Order of H.E. Justice Nassir Al Nasser, the set aside application was granted.

48.text: 18. On 24 April 2025, the DIFC approved the lease termination request from the Claimant.

49.text: 19. In May 2025, the Defendants fi led their defence, with the Claimant subsequently fi ling their reply.

50.text: 20. On 8 September 2025, a Case Management Conference was held before H.E. Deputy Chief Justice Ali Al Madhani. The Court advised that the Defendants proceed with a Part 24 Immediate Judgment applicat...

52.text: 21. Under RDC 24.1, the Court may grant an Immediate Judgment on the whole or part of a claim if: “(1) it considers that

53.text: (a) the Claimant has no real prospect of succeeding on the claim or issue; or …; and

54.text: (2) there is no other compelling reason why the case or issue should be disposed of at a trial.” [emphasis added].

55.text: 22. The scope of “prospect of succeeding” follows the same established approach as the English legal precedent of Swain v. Hillman [2001] 2 All ER 91, being that the prospect must be ‘realistic’ as op...

56.text: 23. A ‘compelling reason’ amounts to ‘circumstances that ought to be investigated’ (Miles v. Bull [1969] 1 QB 258 at 266A). As this application type operates in the negative, it must be demonstrated t...

57.text: 24. The Defendants also rely on the overriding objective as to the compelling reason. It is submitted that granting the Application would save on costs and time.

58.text: 25. The legal burden of proof in the Application rests on the Defendants to show that the Claimant has no real prospect and no other compelling reason to continue its claim against D2 and D3, however ...

110.text: 51. To summarise, it is the Claimant’s position that the Defendants have failed to reach the threshold of RDC 24.1.

115.text: 55. In particular, I am not satisfi ed that, at this interlocutory stage and prior to full document production (as was available at the time of the Hearing), it is suffi ciently clear whether D2 and D...

116.text: 56. D2 and D3 place signifi cant reliance on the alleged issuance of the Licence as determinative of their personal liability under the Agreement. However, the evidence presently before the Court is c...

117.text: 57. The Court also considers that the structure and wording of the Agreement itself raise issues unsuitable for determination under RDC Part 24. The Agreement defi nes the “Tenants” in the plural, lis...

118.text: 58. Further, the termination correspondence dated 24 June 2024 refers to the original Agreement signed by the parties. The fi nal page of that Agreement bears only the signatures of D2 and D3, with no...

119.text: These features raise legitimate questions as to whether D1 was ever formally substituted into the Agreement as a contracting party in a commercial capacity, or whether D2 and D3 remained bound in a pe...

120.text: 59. The Defendants’ reliance on Articles 31, 49, and 51 of the DIFC Contract Law further underscores the unsuitability of this Application for immediate judgment. The application of those provisions, ...

121.text: 60. The Court therefore accepts the Claimant’s submission that this case is not ready for determination under RDC 24.1. There are matters which ought to be investigated and the presence of real disagr...

122.text: 61. Accordingly, the Court fi nds that the Defendants have failed to discharge the burden of establishing that the Claimant has no real prospect of succeeding against D2 and D3, or that there is no ot...

123.text: 62. For these reasons, the Application for Immediate Judgment is dismissed. The issues raised therein shall proceed to trial and be determined alongside the main action.
